1→---
     2→name: swarm-project
     3→description: Shatter-specific swarm extensions. Adds epic/wave-based scheduling via bd swarm CLI and Shatter quality gates. Delegates generic team/worktree/merge mechanics to the global /swarm skill.
     4→user-invocable: true
     5→---
     6→
     7→# Swarm Project: Shatter Extensions
     8→
     9→This skill extends the global `/swarm` skill with Shatter-specific behavior.
    10→**Always run `/swarm` first** — this skill adds epic mode and project-specific
    11→quality gates on top.
    12→
    13→## Epic Mode
    14→
    15→When given an epic ID instead of (or in addition to) individual issue IDs,
    16→use wave-based scheduling instead of flat `bd ready` triage.
    17→
    18→### Phase 1 override — Epic Triage
    19→
    20→1. Run `bd swarm validate <epic-id>` to analyze the DAG structure
    21→   - Computes ready fronts (waves), detects cycles, inversions, disconnected subgraphs
    22→   - If validation fails (exit code 1), report errors and stop
    23→2. Run `bd swarm create <epic-id>` to register the swarm molecule (skip if
    24→   `bd swarm list` already shows one for this epic)
    25→3. Use wave 0 (first ready front) as batch 1. Later waves are the wait queue.
    26→4. Present wave structure to user for approval, then hand off to `/swarm` Phase 2+
    27→
    28→### Phase 4 override — Wave Draining
    29→
    30→When wave N is fully merged, run `bd swarm status <epic-id>` to confirm which
    31→issues are now unblocked, then spawn teammates for the next wave's ready issues.
    32→
    33→### Phase 6 addition — Epic Close
    34→
    35→Run `bd swarm status <epic-id>` one final time to confirm all children are
    36→closed. Close the swarm molecule if all work is done.
    37→
    38→## Quality Gates (Phase 5)
    39→
    40→These override the generic "run project quality gates" step in global `/swarm`:
    41→
    42→1. `/check-all` — full cross-language quality gate (Rust clippy + tests, TS tests, Go tests)
    43→2. `/walkthrough-review` — verify the demo walkthrough covers new functionality
    44→   and output is human-readable. Update walkthrough if needed.
    45→

1→# Flotsam — Claude Instructions
     2→
     3→Personal knowledge management system: a monorepo with a Go API (`api/`) and a
     4→React/TypeScript frontend (`web/`). Component-specific deep-dives are in the
     5→`@import` files below.
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## Prerequisites
    10→
    11→- **Go** 1.24+
    12→- **Node.js** 22+ with **pnpm** 9+ (`npm install -g pnpm`)
    13→- **Docker** (for `make dev` — runs PostgreSQL + pgvector + MinIO in containers)
    14→- **PostgreSQL** 16+ with pgvector (only if managing DB manually instead of `make dev`)
    15→- **golangci-lint** (optional): see golangci-lint docs
    16→
    17→---
    18→
    19→## Environment setup
    20→
    21→```bash
    22→cp .env.example .env   # fill in DATABASE_URL and JWT_SECRET at minimum
    23→```
    24→
    25→Required: `DATABASE_URL` — the API refuses to start without it.
    26→
    27→Ports:
    28→- **API**: `:8081`
    29→- **Web dev server**: `:8080` — proxies `/graphql`, `/api`, and `/mcp` → `localhost:8081`
    30→
    31→---
    32→
    33→## Commands (Root Makefile)
    34→
    35→| Command | Action |
    36→|---|---|
    37→| `make dev` | Full-stack dev environment (Docker PG + air + Vite) — single command |
    38→| `make dev-db-up` | Start dev database only (PG 16 + pgvector on port 5433) |
    39→| `make dev-db-down` | Stop dev database |
    40→| `make api-build` | Compile `bin/flotsam-api` |
    41→| `make api-run` | Run API server |
    42→| `make api-generate` | Regenerate gqlgen code |
    43→| `make api-test` | All Go tests (race detector) |
    44→| `make api-test-unit` | Unit tests only (`-short`) |
    45→| `make lint` | Run all linters (Go + web) |
    46→| `make api-lint` | go vet + golangci-lint (includes gosec) |
    47→| `make api-migrate-up` | Apply pending migrations |
    48→| `make api-migrate-down` | Roll back last migration |
    49→| `make web-install` | `pnpm install` |
    50→| `make web-dev` | Vite dev server (port 8080) |
    51→| `make web-build` | TypeScript check + production build |
    52→| `make web-lint` | ESLint (zero-warning mode) |
    53→| `make web-test` | Vitest unit tests |
    54→| `make web-schema-sync` | Regenerate `web/schema.graphql` + `graphql-env.d.ts` from API SDL |
    55→| `make test-quick` | Fast checks: Go unit tests + TS build + ESLint (~10-20s, no DB) |
    56→| `make test-standard` | Full local: quick + web unit tests + Go lint (~30-60s, no DB) |
    57→| `make test-full` | Complete: standard + integration + e2e (requires DB + services) |
    58→
    59→---
    60→
    61→## Testing standards
    62→
    63→### Test tiers
    64→
    65→| Tier | Command | Time | External deps | Use when |
    66→|---|---|---|---|---|
    67→| Quick | `make test-quick` | ~10-20s | None | During development |
    68→| Standard | `make test-standard` | ~30-60s | None | Before committing |
    69→| Full | `make test-full` | ~2-5min | DB + running services | Before merge |
    70→
    71→### Core rules
    72→
    73→- **Always write tests for new additions.** Tests ship with the code, not as a follow-up.
    74→- **Update existing tests when modifying behaviour.**
    75→- **Target >= 80% line coverage per package.** Glue/wiring packages (`cmd/server`, `router`) are exempt.
    76→- **Always run database code against a real database.** Compilation and unit tests alone are not sufficient — runtime issues like pgx type encoding mismatches only surface with a live connection.
    77→
    78→---
    79→
    80→## Issue tracking (Beads)
    81→
    82→```bash
    83→bd list                                # all open issues
    84→bd ready                               # issues with no blockers (ready to work)
    85→bd show <id>                           # detailed view with dependencies
    86→bd create --title "..." --description "..."
    87→bd update <id> --status in_progress
    88→bd close <id>
    89→```
    90→
    91→See `AGENTS.md` for issue type guidance, epic creation, dependencies, and
    92→completion workflow.
    93→
    94→---
    95→
    96→## Common task recipes
    97→
    98→### Add a config env var
    99→
   100→1. Add field to `Config` struct in `api/internal/config/config.go` with `env:"VAR_NAME"` tag
   101→2. Document it in `.env.example`
   102→3. Access via `cfg.FieldName` (config flows through `router.Deps`)
   103→
   104→### Add a database query
   105→
   106→Use pgx directly: `pool.QueryRow(ctx, sql, $1, $2, ...)`. Always pass context.
   107→Never build SQL with `fmt.Sprintf` — always use `$N` placeholders.
   108→
   109→### Add a Go package
   110→
   111→Create `api/internal/<name>/` with `package <name>`. Pass deps explicitly via
   112→structs; no `init()` globals.
   113→
   114→---
   115→
   116→## Git workflow
   117→
   118→When work is complete and verified, merge directly into `main` from the command
   119→line. Do not create pull requests unless there is an unresolvable merge problem.
   120→
   121→See `AGENTS.md` for commit conventions, worktree workflow, and session
   122→completion protocol.
   123→
   124→---
   125→
   126→## Implementation plans
   127→
   128→After plan mode approval, copy the plan from `~/.claude/plans/` into
   129→`docs/plans/` with a meaningful name (e.g., `flt-a1b-db-schema.md`).
   130→Plans in `~/.claude/` are machine-local; the repo copy syncs across devices.
   131→See `AGENTS.md` for naming conventions and lifecycle.
   132→
   133→---
   134→
   135→## Project specs (`docs/specs/`)
   136→
   137→Comprehensive product and architecture documentation lives in `docs/specs/`.
   138→Consult these before making significant changes to understand current behaviour.
   139→
   140→| File | Covers |
   141→|---|---|
   142→| `product-overview.md` | Architecture, data model, API design, auth, MCP, tech choices |
   143→
   144→---
   145→
   146→## What NOT to do
   147→
   148→- **Never edit** `graph/generated/generated.go` or `graph/model/models_gen.go` — run `make api-generate`
   149→- **Never commit** `.env` — only `.env.example`
   150→- **Never use `fmt.Sprintf` to build SQL** — use parameterized `$N` placeholders
   151→- **Never hardcode brand colors** — use Mantine color refs or CSS vars
   152→- **Never use Tailwind utility classes** — use Mantine style props or inline styles
   153→- **Never add** `node_modules/` or `dist/` to git
   154→
   155→---
   156→
   157→## README.md
   158→
   159→`README.md` is the human-facing project documentation. **Keep it up to date**
   160→when making changes that affect how someone builds, runs, or configures the
   161→project.
   162→
   163→---
   164→
   165→@api/CLAUDE.md
   166→@web/CLAUDE.md
   167→

1→---
     2→name: swarm
     3→description: Work all ready beads issues in parallel using agent teams with worktree isolation. Triages issues, spawns teammates, reviews plans, merges results, and runs quality gates.
     4→user-invocable: true
     5→---
     6→
     7→# Swarm: Parallel Issue Work
     8→
     9→Work `bd ready` issues in parallel using agent teams. Each teammate works in an
    10→isolated worktree with plan-mode supervision.
    11→
    12→## Usage
    13→
    14→- `/swarm` — work all `bd ready` issues
    15→- `/swarm kapow-abc kapow-def` — work specific issues only
    16→
    17→---
    18→
    19→## Phase 1 — Triage
    20→
    21→1. Run `bd ready` (or use the provided issue IDs)
    22→2. Run `bd show <id>` for each issue to understand scope
    23→3. Identify issues that touch **overlapping files** — these must be sequenced, not parallelized
    24→4. Skip issues that are too large or ambiguous for a single teammate (flag for manual work)
    25→5. Present the triage plan to the user:
    26→   - Which issues will be parallelized
    27→   - Which issues are sequenced and why
    28→   - Which issues are skipped and why
    29→
    30→**Wait for user approval before proceeding.**
    31→
    32→---
    33→
    34→## Phase 2 — Team Setup
    35→
    36→1. Create a team via `TeamCreate` (name: `swarm-YYYY-MM-DD` or similar)
    37→2. Create a task (via `TaskCreate`) for each parallelizable issue, including the beads ID in the task description
    38→3. For each task, spawn a teammate via the `Agent` tool with:
    39→   - `subagent_type: "general-purpose"`
    40→   - `mode: "plan"`
    41→   - `team_name: <team>`
    42→   - Do **NOT** use `isolation: "worktree"` — it is unreliable. Teammates
    43→     create their own worktrees as their mandatory first step (see below).
    44→4. Each teammate's prompt must include:
    45→   - The full issue details (from `bd show`)
    46→   - Instruction to run the appropriate quality gate before reporting done:
    47→     - Go changes → `make api-test-unit && make api-lint`
    48→     - Web changes → `cd web && pnpm build && pnpm lint && pnpm test`
    49→     - Both → `make test-standard`
    50→     - GraphQL schema changes → `make api-generate && make web-schema-sync` before anything else
    51→   - Instruction to **push their branch** before reporting completion
    52→   - Instruction to follow CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md conventions
    53→   - **Worktree isolation rules** (copy verbatim into each prompt, replacing
    54→     `<AGENT_NAME>` with the teammate's name):
    55→     ```
    56→     ## WORKTREE ISOLATION — CRITICAL
    57→
    58→     ### Create your worktree (MANDATORY FIRST STEP)
    59→     Before doing ANY other work, create and enter your worktree:
    60→
    61→     ```bash
    62→     WORKTREE_NAME="<AGENT_NAME>"
    63→     REPO_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
    64→     WORKTREE_DIR="$REPO_ROOT/.claude/worktrees/$WORKTREE_NAME"
    65→     BRANCH_NAME="worktree/$WORKTREE_NAME"
    66→
    67→     # Create worktree (if it already exists, just cd into it)
    68→     if [ -d "$WORKTREE_DIR" ]; then
    69→       echo "Worktree already exists, reusing"
    70→     else
    71→       git worktree add "$WORKTREE_DIR" -b "$BRANCH_NAME"
    72→     fi
    73→
    74→     cd "$WORKTREE_DIR"
    75→     ```
    76→
    77→     Then verify you are in the right place:
    78→
    79→     ```bash
    80→     echo "=== WORKTREE VERIFICATION ===" && \
    81→     echo "CWD: $(pwd)" && \
    82→     echo "Branch: $(git branch --show-current)" && \
    83→     if echo "$(pwd)" | grep -q '\.claude/worktrees/'; then \
    84→       echo "✓ WORKTREE VERIFIED"; \
    85→     else \
    86→       echo "✗ FATAL: NOT in a worktree! CWD=$(pwd)"; \
    87→       echo "You MUST stop and report this error to the team lead."; \
    88→       exit 1; \
    89→     fi
    90→     ```
    91→
    92→     If the verification FAILS, do NOT proceed. Report the error to the team lead
    93→     immediately via SendMessage and stop all work.
    94→
    95→     ### Rules
    96→     1. NEVER cd to the main repo — stay in your worktree directory
    97→     2. NEVER run `git checkout` to switch branches — you are already on your branch
    98→     3. ALWAYS use relative paths or `$(pwd)` for all operations
    99→     4. Before EVERY `git commit` and `git push`, re-verify your CWD is in `.claude/worktrees/`
   100→     5. Commit and push ONLY on your worktree branch — never touch main
   101→     6. When running `make` targets, run them from your worktree root (your CWD)
   102→     7. If ANY command output shows your CWD changed to the main repo, STOP
   103→        immediately, cd back to your worktree, and re-verify
   104→
   105→     ### How to push your branch
   106→     ```bash
   107→     git add <files>
   108→     git commit -m "your message"
   109→     git push origin HEAD  # pushes your worktree branch, NOT main
   110→     ```
   111→     NEVER run `git push origin main` or `git push origin HEAD:main`.
   112→     ```
   113→
   114→---
   115→
   116→## Phase 3 — Plan Review
   117→
   118→For each teammate that submits a plan:
   119→
   120→1. **Verify worktree isolation first**: Before reviewing the plan content, check
   121→   that the teammate's plan mentions their worktree path and branch name. If the
   122→   plan references `/home/ketan/project/kapow` as the working directory (without
   123→   `.claude/worktrees/`), REJECT the plan with feedback to re-run the pre-flight
   124→   check and confirm they are in their worktree.
   125→2. Review the plan for:
   126→   - Correct scope (matches the issue, no scope creep)
   127→   - Quality gates included
   128→   - Test coverage plan (tests are comprehensive and well-targeted)
   129→   - No overlap with other teammates' work
   130→3. Approve or reject via `plan_approval_response`
   131→4. If rejecting, provide specific feedback on what to change
   132→
   133→---
   134→
   135→## Phase 4 — Monitor & Merge
   136→
   137→As teammates complete work:
   138→
   139→1. **Verify quality**: Confirm the teammate ran quality gates and they passed
   140→2. **Get the branch**: Read the worktree branch name from the Agent result
   141→3. **Merge to main**:
   142→   ```bash
   143→   git checkout main
   144→   git merge <branch> --no-edit
   145→   ```
   146→4. **Handle conflicts**: If merge conflicts arise, resolve them or ask the teammate to rebase
   147→5. **Post-merge regeneration** (if GraphQL schema files changed):
   148→   ```bash
   149→   make api-generate && make web-schema-sync
   150→   ```
   151→6. **Close the beads issue**: `bd close <id>`
   152→7. **Delete the branch**: `git branch -d <branch>`
   153→8. **Only then** send `shutdown_request` to the teammate
   154→
   155→**CRITICAL**: Never shut down a teammate before merging their branch. Worktree
   156→cleanup deletes the branch — unmerged work is lost.
   157→
   158→---
   159→
   160→## Phase 5 — Quality Gates
   161→
   162→After **all** teammates are merged:
   163→
   164→1. Run `make test-standard` for a full quality gate (Go tests + lint + TS build + ESLint + web tests)
   165→2. If any failures, fix them on main directly
   166→3. If the demo walkthrough (`demo/steps.ts`) references changed UI, update it

   170→## Phase 6 — Close Protocol

   172→1. Close any remaining beads issues: `bd close <id1> <id2> ...`
   173→2. Sync and push:

   176→   git pull --rebase
   177→   git push
   178→   git status  # Must show "up to date with origin"
   179→   ```
   180→3. Shut down all teammates (if not already done)

1→# API — Claude Instructions
     2→
     3→Go API server. Module: `github.com/ketang/flotsam/api`. All paths below are
     4→relative to `api/` unless noted.
     5→
     6→---
     7→
     8→## Binaries
     9→
    10→| Binary | Command | Purpose |
    11→|---|---|---|
    12→| `flotsamd` | `cmd/flotsamd/` | API server daemon — HTTP server, GraphQL, MCP. Inserts River jobs but does not process them. |
    13→| `flotsam-worker` | `cmd/flotsam-worker/` | Job processor — polls `river_job` table, executes background work (page fetch, transcription, embedding, classification). Non-daemon: runs jobs and exits, or runs continuously with `--watch`. |
    14→| `flotsam` | `cmd/flotsam/` | CLI client — capture, search, list, voice record. Cross-platform single binary. |
    15→
    16→For simple single-machine deployment, `flotsamd --with-worker` forks `flotsam-worker --watch` as a child process.
    17→
    18→## Startup sequence (`cmd/flotsamd/main.go`)
    19→
    20→1. Load `.env` via godotenv (no-op if absent — dev convenience only)
    21→2. Parse all config from env vars → `internal/config.Config`
    22→3. Set the default `slog` logger (format/level from config)
    23→4. Connect pgxpool with retry → `internal/db.Connect`
    24→5. Initialize S3 storage client → `internal/storage.New`
    25→6. Initialize River client in insert-only mode (enqueue jobs, don't process)
    26→7. Choose JWT validator: HMACValidator (HS256) using `JWT_SECRET`
    27→8. Build chi router with all middleware and routes → `internal/router.New`
    28→9. Optionally fork `flotsam-worker --watch` if `--with-worker` flag is set
    29→10. Start HTTP server; block on SIGINT/SIGTERM; graceful shutdown
    30→
    31→---
    32→
    33→## Package map
    34→
    35→| Package | Purpose |
    36→|---|---|
    37→| `internal/config` | Env var binding via `caarlos0/env` struct tags |
    38→| `internal/auth` | JWT validation, password hashing (bcrypt) |
    39→| `internal/db` | pgxpool connection with retry; goose embedded migration runner |
    40→| `internal/item` | Item CRUD, search (FTS + semantic) |
    41→| `internal/middleware` | RequestID, Logging (slog), Recovery, Auth, RateLimit |
    42→| `internal/router` | Chi router wiring — routes, middleware, GraphQL handler |
    43→| `internal/mcp` | MCP server + per-client authorization |
    44→| `internal/provider` | AI provider interfaces (embedding, transcription, classification) |
    45→| `internal/storage` | S3 client abstraction (MinIO dev / R2 prod) |
    46→| `internal/worker` | River background job definitions (page fetch, transcription, etc.) |
    47→| `internal/server` | `http.Server` with production timeouts; `Shutdown` helper |
    48→| `graph/schema/` | GraphQL SDL files (source of truth) |
    49→| `graph/generated/` | gqlgen output — **never edit manually** |
    50→| `graph/model/` | Generated Go types — **never edit manually** |
    51→| `graph/resolver/` | Hand-written resolver implementations |
    52→| `migrations/` | Goose SQL files + `//go:embed` wrapper (`migrations.go`) |
    53→
    54→---
    55→
    56→## Go conventions
    57→
    58→- **Error wrapping**: `fmt.Errorf("package: context: %w", err)` — always include package prefix
    59→- **Logging**: `slog.Error("msg", "key", val, "error", err)` — structured, never `fmt.Println`
    60→- **Constructors**: return `(T, error)`; caller decides how to handle; never panic on bad input
    61→- **Context**: always the first parameter — `func Foo(ctx context.Context, ...)`
    62→- **Package names**: single lowercase word matching the directory name
    63→- **File layout**: exported types first, unexported helpers below
    64→- **Interfaces**: named by behaviour (`Validator`, not `IValidator`); defined where consumed
    65→- **Dependencies**: passed explicitly through structs (e.g. `router.Deps`); no `init()` globals
    66→- **SQL**: always parameterized (`$1`, `$2`); never `fmt.Sprintf` into a query string
    67→
    68→---
    69→
    70→## Authentication (`internal/auth`)
    71→
    72→`Validator` interface with `HMACValidator` (HS256, `JWT_SECRET`).
    73→
    74→### Request flow
    75→
    76→1. **Auth middleware** (`middleware.Auth`) extracts `Authorization: Bearer <token>`,
    77→   validates the JWT, and stores `*auth.Claims` in the request context.
    78→2. Resolvers access the authenticated user via `auth.GetClaims(ctx)`.
    79→
    80→### Protected routes
    81→
    82→- `/graphql` — **OptionalAuth** (anonymous allowed for health; auth required for mutations)
    83→- `/mcp/*` — **Auth** (mandatory JWT or API key; 401 if missing/invalid)
    84→- `/upload` — **Auth** (mandatory JWT)
    85→- `/health` — no auth
    86→
    87→---
    88→
    89→## GraphQL (`graph/`, gqlgen — schema-first)
    90→
    91→### Workflow for adding a query or type
    92→
    93→1. Edit or create a `.graphql` file under `graph/schema/`
    94→2. `make generate` — regenerates `graph/generated/generated.go` and `graph/model/models_gen.go`
    95→3. Implement the new resolver method in `graph/resolver/schema.resolvers.go`
    96→4. Add new deps to the `Resolver` struct in `graph/resolver/resolver.go` and wire them in `router.go`
    97→
    98→---
    99→
   100→## Database & migrations
   101→
   102→- **PostgreSQL 16+** with pgvector
   103→- **Driver**: `jackc/pgx/v5` + `pgxpool` — context-aware, async
   104→- **Migrations**: `pressly/goose/v3` embedded in binary via `migrations/migrations.go`
   105→
   106→### Creating a migration
   107→
   108→```bash
   109→make migrate-create NAME=short_description
   110→# → creates migrations/NNNN_short_description.sql
   111→```
   112→
   113→Write the migration using Goose markers:
   114→
   115→```sql
   116→-- +goose Up
   117→ALTER TABLE items ADD COLUMN foo text;
   118→
   119→-- +goose Down
   120→ALTER TABLE items DROP COLUMN foo;
   121→```
   122→
   123→Then apply:
   124→
   125→```bash
   126→make migrate-up
   127→```
   128→
   129→---
   130→
   131→## Background jobs (River)
   132→
   133→PG-backed job queue using [River](https://riverqueue.com). Jobs are defined in
   134→`internal/worker/` and processed by `flotsam-worker`.
   135→
   136→**Architecture**: `flotsamd` inserts jobs (River client in insert-only mode).
   137→`flotsam-worker` processes them (River client with workers registered).
   138→They share the same PostgreSQL database — no message broker needed.
   139→
   140→### Job types
   141→
   142→| Job | Purpose | Trigger |
   143→|---|---|---|
   144→| PageFetch | Fetch + extract readable content from bookmark URLs | Bookmark capture |
   145→| Transcribe | Transcribe audio via Whisper API or local whisper.cpp | Voice note upload |
   146→| Embed | Generate vector embedding for item content | Item create/update |
   147→| Classify | Auto-extract metadata (category, people, topics) via LLM | After content ready |
   148→| S3Upload | Upload large content to S3 | File/audio upload |
   149→
   150→### Running the worker
   151→
   152→```bash
   153→flotsam-worker --watch          # continuous mode: poll for jobs, run until killed
   154→flotsam-worker --once           # run pending jobs and exit (useful for cron/debugging)
   155→```
   156→
   157→### Adding a job
   158→
   159→1. Create `internal/worker/<job_name>.go` with a struct implementing `river.Worker`
   160→2. Register the worker in `internal/worker/registry.go`
   161→3. Enqueue from service layer (in `flotsamd`): `riverClient.Insert(ctx, &MyJobArgs{...})`
   162→
   163→---
   164→
   165→## Provider interfaces (`internal/provider`)
   166→
   167→Pluggable AI service interfaces with local fallback for sensitive content:
   168→
   169→```go
   170→type EmbeddingProvider interface {
   171→    Embed(ctx context.Context, text string) ([]float32, error)
   172→    EmbedBatch(ctx context.Context, texts []string) ([][]float32, error)
   173→    Dimensions() int
   174→}
   175→
   176→type TranscriptionProvider interface {
   177→    Transcribe(ctx context.Context, audio io.Reader, opts TranscribeOpts) (string, error)
   178→}
   179→
   180→type ClassificationProvider interface {
   181→    Classify(ctx context.Context, content string) (*Classification, error)
   182→}
   183→```
   184→
   185→Routing: if item sensitivity >= `sensitive`, use local providers (Ollama).
   186→Otherwise, use configured default (OpenAI).
   187→
   188→---
   189→
   190→## Testing
   191→
   192→### Rules
   193→
   194→- **Target >= 80% line coverage per package** via unit tests (`-short`). Document exceptions.
   195→- **Never touch a real DB in unit tests.** Use `testing.Short()` guards in integration tests.
   196→- **Write tests alongside code**, not as a follow-up.
   197→
   198→### Unit vs integration
   199→
   200→- Unit tests: no DB, no network; run via `make test-unit` (`-short`)
   201→- Integration tests: name prefixed `Integration`; skip when `testing.Short()` is true or `DATABASE_URL` is unset
   202→- New GraphQL queries require: resolver unit tests **and** an integration test
   203→
   204→### Per-package expectations
   205→
   206→| Package | Unit coverage | Notes |
   207→|---|---|---|
   208→| `internal/item` | >= 80% | Core logic |
   209→| `internal/auth` | >= 80% | |
   210→| `internal/config` | >= 80% | |
   211→| `internal/provider` | >= 80% | Test with mocked APIs |
   212→| `cmd/server`, `internal/router` | exempt | Wiring-only; covered by integration tests |
   213→

1→# Web Frontend — Claude Instructions
     2→
     3→React 18 + TypeScript + Vite frontend. All paths below are relative to `web/`
     4→unless noted. Dev server runs on `:8080` and proxies `/graphql`, `/api`, and
     5→`/mcp` to the API at `localhost:8081`.
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## Mantine theme
    10→
    11→The Mantine theme is configured in `src/theme.ts` and applied via `MantineProvider`
    12→in `main.tsx`. Brand colors are defined as 10-shade tuples.
    13→
    14→### Using brand colors
    15→
    16→Use Mantine's color system to reference brand colors:
    17→
    18→```tsx
    19→<Title c="brand.9">Heading</Title>
    20→<Button color="accent">Action</Button>
    21→<Box bg="brand.0">Light background</Box>
    22→```
    23→
    24→**Never hardcode hex values** — use Mantine color references or CSS variables.
    25→
    26→---
    27→
    28→## Styling approach
    29→
    30→Use Mantine style props (`bg`, `c`, `p`, `m`, `fz`, `fw`, etc.) and layout
    31→components (`Container`, `Group`, `Stack`, `SimpleGrid`, `Box`). For one-off
    32→styles, use the `style` prop. Do **not** use Tailwind utility classes.
    33→
    34→PostCSS is configured with `postcss-preset-mantine` and `postcss-simple-vars`.
    35→
    36→---
    37→
    38→## Adding a page
    39→
    40→1. Create `src/pages/NewPage.tsx` — use a named export (`export function NewPage`)
    41→2. Add the route in `src/App.tsx`
    42→
    43→---
    44→
    45→## GraphQL queries (gql.tada) — MANDATORY
    46→
    47→**Every GraphQL query and mutation MUST use `graphql()` from `gql.tada`.** Raw
    48→template-string queries are **forbidden** — they bypass compile-time schema
    49→validation.
    50→
    51→### Correct pattern
    52→
    53→```tsx
    54→import { graphql } from 'gql.tada'
    55→import { useQuery } from '@/lib/graphql'
    56→
    57→const MyQuery = graphql(`query MyQuery { field1 field2 }`)
    58→const [result] = useQuery({ query: MyQuery })  // types inferred from schema
    59→```
    60→
    61→### Schema sync
    62→
    63→After any change to `api/graph/schema/*.graphql`, regenerate:
    64→
    65→```bash
    66→make web-schema-sync   # from project root
    67→```
    68→
    69→---
    70→
    71→## Testing (Vitest)
    72→
    73→Unit tests use Vitest with `happy-dom` environment. Coverage thresholds enforced
    74→at 80%. New components require a test file alongside them.
    75→
    76→Use `renderWithMantine()` from `src/test/render.tsx` when testing components
    77→that use Mantine.
    78→
    79→### Build verification (mandatory)
    80→
    81→**Every change to frontend code must be verified with a full build before it is
    82→considered done.** Run:
    83→
    84→```bash
    85→pnpm build   # tsc -b && vite build — must finish with zero errors
    86→pnpm lint    # eslint --max-warnings 0 — must finish with zero warnings
    87→```
    88→
    89→---
    90→
    91→## Linting
    92→
    93→**Zero warnings policy** — `pnpm lint` runs `eslint . --max-warnings 0`.
    94→Path alias `@/` maps to `src/`.
    95→

1→---
     2→name: walkthrough-review
     3→description: Run the demo walkthrough and evaluate its output for human readability. Use after changing CLI output, explore formatting, log levels, or protocol logging.
     4→user-invocable: true
     5→---
     6→
     7→## Purpose
     8→
     9→Evaluate whether `demo/walkthrough.sh` output is useful and readable for a human user. Run this after any change that affects what shatter prints — explore reports, log formatting, verbosity flags, cluster summaries, etc.
    10→
    11→## What a human wants from this output
    12→
    13→A developer running shatter is asking: **"What does this function actually do, and are there any surprises?"**
    14→
    15→The output should answer these questions, in priority order:
    16→
    17→### 1. How many distinct behaviors does this function have?
    18→
    19→The path count is the headline. A function with 2 paths is simple; one with 12 might be hiding complexity. This number should be immediately visible.
    20→
    21→### 2. What input conditions trigger each behavior?
    22→
    23→Constraints on the function's actual parameter names, in declaration order. Express constraints with mathematical precision and conciseness — `b = 0`, `n < 0`, `s.length > 10 && s[0] = "/"` — not natural language paraphrases and not raw solver output.
    24→
    25→### 3. What is the outcome of each behavior?
    26→
    27→For each path, one of:
    28→- **Return value**: the concrete value or a description of the return expression
    29→- **Thrown error**: the error/exception type in languages with typed errors (e.g., `Error`, `TypeError`, `ValueError`, `std::io::Error`), or the error value in languages where errors are scalars (e.g., Go's `error` string, Rust's enum variant). Include the error message if it's a fixed string.
    30→- **Side effects**: if the path's primary observable effect is a side effect rather than a return
    31→
    32→### 4. Concrete examples per path
    33→
    34→2-3 representative input/output pairs per path cluster. Arguments shown with their parameter names in declaration order. These ground the abstract constraints in specific values a developer can paste into a REPL.
    35→
    36→### 5. Are there surprises?
    37→
    38→Paths the developer likely didn't anticipate should stand out: unhandled edge cases, implicit type coercions, unexpected nulls/NaN, unreachable code that turned out to be reachable. These are the primary value of exploratory testing.
    39→
    40→### 6. How much of the input space does each path cover?
    41→
    42→Is the error case a narrow edge (measure-zero like `b = 0`) or half the domain (`n < 0`)? This tells the developer whether a path is a corner case or a primary behavior.
    43→
    44→### 7. Exploration completeness
    45→
    46→Did exploration exhaust the path space, or hit iteration/timeout limits with unexplored branches remaining? The human needs to know if the map is complete or partial.
    47→
    48→### 8. Batch triage (multi-function runs)
    49→
    50→When exploring multiple functions, a one-line-per-function summary lets the developer scan and triage: which functions are simple (2 paths, no errors) and which are complex or problematic.
    51→
    52→## Procedure
    53→
    54→### 1. Run the walkthrough and capture output
    55→
    56→```bash
    57→./demo/walkthrough.sh --auto --delay 0 2>&1 | tee /tmp/shatter-walkthrough-review.txt
    58→```
    59→
    60→If the walkthrough fails to run (build errors, missing examples), report that and stop — fixing the build is a prerequisite.
    61→
    62→### 2. Read the captured output and evaluate against each criterion
    63→
    64→Go through the criteria below one by one. For each, quote the specific lines that pass or fail.
    65→
    66→### 3. Report findings and suggest fixes
    67→
    68→For each failing criterion:
    69→- Quote the offending output (first few lines, not the whole block)
    70→- Explain what's wrong
    71→- Suggest a specific fix: which file, which function, what change
    72→
    73→## Evaluation Criteria
    74→
    75→### A. No protocol noise at INFO level
    76→
    77→**Fail** if the output at default verbosity contains:
    78→- Raw JSON objects (`{"protocol_version":...}`, `{"id":...,"command":...}`)
    79→- Lines prefixed with `[shatter-ts]`, `[shatter-go]`, or `[shatter-rust]`
    80→- Full stack traces (multi-line `at Function...` / `at Object...` blocks)
    81→
    82→These belong at TRACE level only.
    83→
    84→### B. Every function has a summary header
    85→
    86→For each explored function, the output must include:
    87→- The function name
    88→- Its source file and line number
    89→- The number of distinct paths discovered
    90→
    91→**Fail** if a function's results appear without this context, or if the only output is a bare "Exploration complete: N iterations" line with no per-function breakdown.
    92→
    93→### C. Path conditions use parameter names and mathematical notation
    94→
    95→Each path should be described by constraints on the function's actual parameter names, in declaration order. Constraints should be mathematically precise and concise.
    96→
    97→**Fail** if:
    98→- Constraints use generic names like `arg0`, `arg1` instead of actual parameter names
    99→- Constraints are verbose natural language ("when the second argument is equal to zero")
   100→- Constraints are raw solver output (`(= (_ bv0 64) arg1)`)
   101→- Parameters appear in an order different from the function signature
   102→
   103→### D. Error paths describe the error type or value
   104→
   105→When a path throws or returns an error:
   106→- In typed-error languages (TypeScript, Java, Python, Rust): show the error/exception type (`Error`, `TypeError`, `DivisionByZero`)
   107→- In scalar-error languages (Go): show the error string or value
   108→- Include the error message if it's a fixed string
   109→
   110→**Fail** if errors are described only as "throws an error" or "returns an error" without the type/value.
   111→
   112→### E. Examples are concrete and selective
   113→
   114→Each path cluster should show 2-3 concrete input/output examples with parameter names in declaration order.
   115→
   116→**Fail** if:
   117→- A cluster shows no examples
   118→- A cluster dumps every execution (10+ examples)
   119→- Examples lack actual values (showing placeholders or types instead)
   120→- Parameter names are missing from examples
   121→
   122→### F. Output volume is proportional to function count, not iteration count
   123→
   124→A function explored with 100 iterations should produce roughly the same output as one explored with 20 (assuming similar path count). Iteration count is an engine detail.
   125→
   126→**Fail** if total output lines scale with iteration count rather than the number of functions and paths. Guideline: fewer than 20 lines per function at INFO level.
   127→
   128→### G. Performance data only when requested
   129→
   130→Performance stats (timing, memory, iteration counts) should appear only when `--perf` is passed.
   131→
   132→**Fail** if performance data appears in default output. If `--perf` is not yet implemented, note as "not yet applicable."
   133→
   134→### H. Console-appropriate formatting
   135→
   136→**Fail** if the output contains:
   137→- Timestamps on every line
   138→- `[INFO]` / `[DEBUG]` / `[WARN]` prefixes at the default log level
   139→- Machine-readable structured output where human-readable text is expected
   140→
   141→### I. Batch triage summary
   142→
   143→When multiple functions are explored in one invocation, the output should include a scannable summary — one line per function with path count and whether errors were found.
   144→
   145→**Fail** if the only way to assess multiple functions is to read through the full detail of each one sequentially.
   146→
   147→### J. Completeness signal
   148→
   149→The output should indicate whether exploration is complete (all paths found) or partial (hit limits).
   150→
   151→**Fail** if there's no way to distinguish "3 paths, fully explored" from "3 paths found before hitting the iteration cap."
   152→
   153→### K. No errors, crashes, or skipped functions due to bugs
   154→
   155→The walkthrough is also a correctness gate. Check the ERROR SUMMARY at the end of the output, and scan for:
   156→
   157→- `[error]` lines in stderr (exploration errors, deserialization failures, frontend crashes)
   158→- `Command exited with status N` (unexpected non-zero exit codes)
   159→- Functions skipped due to errors (e.g., "skipped: error: exploration error: ...")
   160→- `failed to deserialize`, `panic`, `SIGSEGV`, or similar crash indicators
   161→
   162→**Expected failures** (do NOT count as errors):
   163→- `stale` command exit code 1 (means "some functions are stale" — informational, not a failure)
   164→- Scan errors for `11-opaque-types.ts` and `12-external-deps.ts` (opaque types, missing external modules)
   165→
   166→**Fail** if any step produces unexpected errors, crashes, or skips functions that should succeed. File a beads issue for each distinct failure.
   167→
   168→## Output Format
   169→
   170→Structure your report as:
   171→
   172→```
   173→## Walkthrough Review
   174→
   175→**Overall**: PASS / MIXED / FAIL
   176→
   177→### A. No protocol noise at INFO level — PASS/FAIL
   178→[details]
   179→
   180→### B. Every function has a summary header — PASS/FAIL
   181→[details]
   182→
   183→...
   184→
   185→## Suggested Fixes
   186→1. [file:line] — [what to change and why]
   187→2. ...
   188→```
   189→

1→---
     2→name: pre-completion
     3→description: Verify all completion criteria before declaring work done. Runs quality gates, E2E tests, checks walkthrough requirements, verifies commits are pushed, and confirms no scope creep.
     4→user-invocable: true
     5→---
     6→
     7→# Pre-Completion: Done Criteria Verification
     8→
     9→Verify all completion criteria are met before announcing work is done to the
    10→team lead (if in a team) or the user (if working solo).
    11→**Do not declare completion until this skill passes.**
    12→
    13→**If any check fails, you are NOT done.** Fix the failing issue, then re-run
    14→`/pre-completion` from the top. Repeat until all checks pass. Do not announce
    15→completion, send a completion message, or report to the lead with any FAIL
    16→status — go back and fix it first.
    17→
    18→---
    19→
    20→## Phase 1 — Determine What Changed
    21→
    22→1. **Identify changed files**:
    23→   ```bash
    24→   git diff --name-only main...HEAD
    25→   ```
    26→   If no commits yet vs main, use `git diff --name-only HEAD` for staged/unstaged changes.
    27→
    28→2. **Classify changes** into categories:
    29→   - `rust-core` — files in `shatter-core/`
    30→   - `rust-cli` — files in `shatter-cli/`
    31→   - `typescript` — files in `shatter-ts/`
    32→   - `go` — files in `shatter-go/`
    33→   - `rust-frontend` — files in `shatter-rust/`
    34→   - `protocol` — any `protocol.rs` or `protocol.ts` or `protocol/` files
    35→   - `pipeline` — solver, instrumentor, explorer, orchestrator, or CLI wiring
    36→   - `cli-output` — CLI commands, formatting, example files, demo scripts
    37→   - `other` — docs, config, examples
    38→
    39→---
    40→
    41→## Phase 2 — Quality Gates
    42→
    43→Run the appropriate language-specific gates based on what changed, plus E2E tests unconditionally.
    44→
    45→### Language-specific (if the language was touched):
    46→
    47→1. **Rust** (if `rust-core` or `rust-cli` changed):
    48→   ```bash
    49→   cargo test
    50→   cargo clippy -- -D warnings
    51→   ```
    52→
    53→2. **TypeScript** (if `typescript` changed):
    54→   ```bash
    55→   cd shatter-ts && npm test
    56→   cd shatter-ts && npx tsc --noEmit
    57→   ```
    58→
    59→3. **Go** (if `go` changed):
    60→   ```bash
    61→   cd shatter-go && go test ./...
    62→   cd shatter-go && go vet ./...
    63→   ```
    64→
    65→4. **Rust frontend** (if `rust-frontend` changed):
    66→   ```bash
    67→   cd shatter-rust && cargo test
    68→   cd shatter-rust && cargo clippy -- -D warnings
    69→   ```
    70→
    71→### Always required:
    72→
    73→5. **E2E concolic tests** — always run regardless of what changed:
    74→   ```bash
    75→   cargo test --test e2e_concolic
    76→   ```
    77→   These are the only tests that validate the full pipeline end-to-end. A module
    78→   can pass all its own tests while being silently disconnected from the pipeline.
    79→
    80→### Conditional:
    81→
    82→6. **Protocol sync** (if `protocol` changed):
    83→   - Verify protocol types are consistent across all languages.
    84→   - Run cross-language tests (Full tier).
    85→
    86→7. **Walkthrough** (if `cli-output` changed):
    87→   ```bash
    88→   bash demo/walkthrough.sh --auto --delay 0
    89→   ```
    90→   Errors in steps 1-19 indicate regressions — fix before proceeding.
    91→
    92→---
    93→
    94→## Phase 3 — Code Quality Checks
    95→
    96→1. **No magic numbers/string literals**: Verify constants are defined, not inline literals.
    97→2. **No `unwrap()` in library code** (Rust): Verify `Result` and `?` are used.
    98→3. **No `any` type** (TypeScript): Verify proper typing throughout.
    99→4. **Parallel code path parity**: If the change touches one of a parallel pair
   100→   (e.g., `buildSymExpr` / `buildSymExprWithFlow`, random explorer / concolic
   101→   orchestrator, CLI wiring for `--concolic` vs default), verify the other path
   102→   handles the same cases.
   103→5. **Bug fix has reproduction test**: If this is a bug fix, verify a test exists
   104→   that would fail without the fix.
   105→
   106→---
   107→
   108→## Phase 4 — Git Status
   109→
   110→1. **All changes committed**:
   111→   ```bash
   112→   git status --porcelain
   113→   ```
   114→   - Working tree must be clean (no uncommitted changes).
   115→   - If there are uncommitted changes, commit them first.
   116→
   117→2. **Branch pushed** (if in a worktree or on a feature branch):
   118→   ```bash
   119→   git log --oneline origin/<branch>..HEAD 2>/dev/null || echo "no upstream"
   120→   ```
   121→   - All commits must be pushed. If unpushed commits exist, push now:
   122→     ```bash
   123→     git push -u origin HEAD
   124→     ```
   125→
   126→3. **Commit messages reference the issue**:
   127→   - Verify commit messages follow `<issue-key>: <description>` format.
   128→
   129→---
   130→
   131→## Phase 5 — Scope Verification
   132→
   133→1. **No scope creep**: Compare the changed files and functionality against the
   134→   original issue description.
   135→   - If you implemented something not in the issue, create a new beads issue for it
   136→     and either revert or note the scope expansion.
   137→   - Extra work discovered during implementation should be filed as new issues,
   138→     not silently included.
   139→
   140→2. **Issue acceptance criteria met**: Re-read the issue description and verify
   141→   every acceptance criterion is satisfied.
   142→
   143→---
   144→
   145→## Output
   146→
   147→Report a summary table. **This table IS the proof of completion.** You MUST
   148→include it verbatim in your completion message to the team lead (via
   149→`SendMessage`) or in your final response to the user. The lead will reject
   150→completion announcements that do not include this table.
   151→
   152→```
   153→| Check                        | Status      | Notes                   |
   154→|------------------------------|-------------|-------------------------|
   155→| Rust tests                   | PASS / FAIL / N/A | ...              |
   156→| Rust clippy                  | PASS / FAIL / N/A | ...              |
   157→| TypeScript tests             | PASS / FAIL / N/A | ...              |
   158→| TypeScript types             | PASS / FAIL / N/A | ...              |
   159→| Go tests                     | PASS / FAIL / N/A | ...              |
   160→| Go vet                       | PASS / FAIL / N/A | ...              |
   161→| Protocol sync                | PASS / FAIL / N/A | ...              |
   162→| E2E concolic                 | PASS / FAIL | ...                     |
   163→| Walkthrough                  | PASS / FAIL / N/A | ...              |
   164→| Parallel path parity         | PASS / N/A  | ...                     |
   165→| Bug repro test exists        | PASS / N/A  | ...                     |
   166→| All changes committed        | PASS / FAIL | ...                     |
   167→| Branch pushed                | PASS / FAIL | ...                     |
   168→| Commit messages valid        | PASS / WARN | ...                     |
   169→| Scope matches issue          | PASS / WARN | ...                     |
   170→| Acceptance criteria met      | PASS / FAIL | ...                     |
   171→
   172→Pre-completion: PASS / FAIL
   173→```
   174→
   175→If any required check is FAIL, set overall status to **FAIL** and list what must
   176→be fixed. **Do not stop here** — go fix the failures, then re-run `/pre-completion`.
   177→You must keep iterating until the table shows `Pre-completion: PASS`. Only then
   178→may you send a completion message.

1→# Agent Instructions
     2→
     3→This project uses **bd** (beads) for issue tracking. Run `bd onboard` to get started.
     4→
     5→## Quick Reference
     6→
     7→```bash
     8→bd ready              # Find available work
     9→bd show <id>          # View issue details
    10→bd update <id> --claim  # Claim work atomically
    11→bd close <id>         # Complete work
    12→bd sync               # Sync with git
    13→```
    14→
    15→## Non-Interactive Shell Commands
    16→
    17→**ALWAYS use non-interactive flags** with file operations to avoid hanging on confirmation prompts.
    18→
    19→```bash
    20→cp -f source dest           # NOT: cp source dest
    21→mv -f source dest           # NOT: mv source dest
    22→rm -f file                  # NOT: rm file
    23→rm -rf directory            # NOT: rm -r directory
    24→```
    25→
    26→## Issue Title Guidelines
    27→
    28→Titles appear in `bd list`, `bd ready`, and terminal window titles where space
    29→is limited. The **first ~20 characters** must be meaningfully descriptive:
    30→
    31→| Bad | Good |
    32→|---|---|
    33→| `Add support for bookmark capture` | `Bookmark capture endpoint` |
    34→| `Update the search to use vectors` | `Vector search integration` |
    35→| `Fix bug where transcription fails` | `Transcription timeout fix` |
    36→
    37→- **Lead with the noun or area** ("Search ...", "Auth ...", "MCP ..."), not filler verbs
    38→- **Under 50 characters** — put detail in `--description`
    39→
    40→## Creating Issues
    41→
    42→Use the correct issue type:
    43→- `epic` — a grouping container for related features/tasks. Not directly workable.
    44→- `feature` — delivers new user-visible or agent-visible capability
    45→- `task` — scaffold, chore, or infrastructure work (e.g., project init, CI setup)
    46→- `bug` — something broken that needs fixing
    47→
    48→### Creating Epics
    49→
    50→Epics must use `--waits-for-gate` so they are blocked until their children complete:
    51→
    52→```bash
    53→bd create "Epic: Feature Area" -t epic -p 2 -l label1,label2 \
    54→  -d "Description of the epic" \
    55→  --waits-for-gate all-children
    56→```
    57→
    58→### Creating Child Issues Under Epics
    59→
    60→Use `--parent` and `--waits-for`:
    61→
    62→```bash
    63→bd create "Implement feature X" -t feature -p 1 \
    64→  --parent flt-abc \
    65→  --waits-for flt-abc \
    66→  -d "Description"
    67→```
    68→
    69→### Dependencies Between Issues
    70→
    71→```bash
    72→bd dep add flt-child flt-dependency   # flt-child is blocked by flt-dependency
    73→```
    74→
    75→Only add dependencies where there is a real technical ordering constraint.
    76→
    77→## Implementation Plans
    78→
    79→After plan mode approval, copy the plan into the repo:
    80→
    81→```bash
    82→cp ~/.claude/plans/<random-name>.md docs/plans/<issue-id>-<short-name>.md
    83→```
    84→
    85→Reference from issues:
    86→
    87→```bash
    88→bd update <id> --notes="Plan: docs/plans/<issue-id>-<short-name>.md"
    89→```
    90→
    91→## Completing an Issue
    92→
    93→An issue is not complete until:
    94→1. All code changes pass quality gates (tests, lint, build)
    95→2. Changes are committed
    96→3. The issue is closed with `bd close <id>`
    97→4. Changes are pushed to remote with `bd sync && git push`
    98→
    99→## Git Workflow
   100→
   101→When work is complete and verified, merge directly into `main`. Only fall back
   102→to a pull request if there is an unresolvable merge conflict.
   103→
   104→### Commit messages and issue references
   105→
   106→When a commit includes changes to beads data, list all affected issue IDs:
   107→
   108→```
   109→Add bookmark capture endpoint and GraphQL schema
   110→
   111→Implements capture mutation with page fetch background job.
   112→
   113→Issues: flt-a1b, flt-c2d
   114→```
   115→
   116→### Worktree workflow
   117→
   118→When working in a git worktree, **always merge the branch back into `main`
   119→before ending the session.**
   120→
   121→**Before merging a worktree branch:**
   122→- Preview conflicts first: `git merge --no-commit --no-ff <branch>` then `git merge --abort`
   123→- Rebase long-lived worktrees onto main before merging
   124→
   125→**After merging a worktree that touches GraphQL schema files:**
   126→- Always regenerate: `make api-generate && make web-schema-sync`
   127→
   128→**Worktree cleanup — do NOT do it manually:**
   129→Claude Code manages worktree lifecycle automatically when invoked with
   130→`--worktree`. **Never** run `git worktree remove` yourself.
   131→
   132→<!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION -->
   133→## Issue Tracking with bd (beads)
   134→
   135→**IMPORTANT**: This project uses **bd (beads)** for ALL issue tracking. Do NOT use markdown TODOs, task lists, or other tracking methods.
   136→
   137→### Why bd?
   138→
   139→- Dependency-aware: Track blockers and relationships between issues
   140→- Version-controlled: Built on Dolt with cell-level merge
   141→- Agent-optimized: JSON output, ready work detection, discovered-from links
   142→- Prevents duplicate tracking systems and confusion
   143→
   144→### Quick Start
   145→
   146→**Check for ready work:**
   147→
   148→```bash
   149→bd ready --json
   150→```
   151→
   152→**Create new issues:**
   153→
   154→```bash
   155→bd create "Issue title" --description="Detailed context" -t bug|feature|task -p 0-4 --json
   156→bd create "Issue title" --description="What this issue is about" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:bd-123 --json
   157→```
   158→
   159→**Claim and update:**
   160→
   161→```bash
   162→bd update <id> --claim --json
   163→bd update bd-42 --priority 1 --json
   164→```
   165→
   166→**Complete work:**
   167→
   168→```bash
   169→bd close bd-42 --reason "Completed" --json
   170→```
   171→
   172→### Issue Types
   173→
   174→- `bug` - Something broken
   175→- `feature` - New functionality
   176→- `task` - Work item (tests, docs, refactoring)
   177→- `epic` - Large feature with subtasks
   178→- `chore` - Maintenance (dependencies, tooling)
   179→
   180→### Priorities
   181→
   182→- `0` - Critical (security, data loss, broken builds)
   183→- `1` - High (major features, important bugs)
   184→- `2` - Medium (default, nice-to-have)
   185→- `3` - Low (polish, optimization)
   186→- `4` - Backlog (future ideas)
   187→
   188→### Workflow for AI Agents
   189→
   190→1. **Check ready work**: `bd ready` shows unblocked issues
   191→2. **Claim your task atomically**: `bd update <id> --claim`
   192→3. **Work on it**: Implement, test, document
   193→4. **Discover new work?** Create linked issue:
   194→   - `bd create "Found bug" --description="Details about what was found" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:<parent-id>`
   195→5. **Complete**: `bd close <id> --reason "Done"`
   196→
   197→### Auto-Sync
   198→
   199→bd automatically syncs with git:
   200→
   201→- Exports to `.beads/issues.jsonl` after changes (5s debounce)
   202→- Imports from JSONL when newer (e.g., after `git pull`)
   203→- No manual export/import needed!
   204→
   205→### Important Rules
   206→
   207→- Use bd for ALL task tracking
   208→- Always use `--json` flag for programmatic use
   209→- Link discovered work with `discovered-from` dependencies
   210→- Check `bd ready` before asking "what should I work on?"
   211→- Do NOT create markdown TODO lists
   212→- Do NOT use external issue trackers
   213→
   214→## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)
   215→
   216→**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.
   217→
   218→**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**
   219→
   220→1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
   221→2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
   222→3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
   223→4. **PUSH TO REMOTE** - This is MANDATORY:
   224→   ```bash
   225→   git pull --rebase
   226→   bd sync
   227→   git push
   228→   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
   229→   ```
   230→5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches
   231→6. **Verify** - All changes committed AND pushed
   232→7. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session
   233→
   234→**CRITICAL RULES:**
   235→- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
   236→- NEVER stop before pushing - that leaves work stranded locally
   237→- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" - YOU must push
   238→- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds
   239→

1→---
     2→name: swarm
     3→description: Work all ready beads issues in parallel using agent teams with worktree isolation. Triages issues, spawns teammates, reviews plans, merges results, and runs quality gates.
     4→user-invocable: true
     5→---
     6→
     7→# Swarm: Parallel Issue Work
     8→
     9→Work `bd ready` issues in parallel using agent teams. Each teammate works in an
    10→isolated worktree with plan-mode supervision.
    11→
    12→## Usage
    13→
    14→- `/swarm` — work all `bd ready` issues
    15→- `/swarm flt-abc flt-def` — work specific issues only
    16→
    17→---
    18→
    19→## Phase 1 — Triage
    20→
    21→1. Run `bd ready` (or use the provided issue IDs)
    22→2. Run `bd show <id>` for each issue to understand scope
    23→3. Identify issues that touch **overlapping files** — these must be sequenced, not parallelized
    24→4. Skip issues that are too large or ambiguous for a single teammate (flag for manual work)
    25→5. Present the triage plan to the user:
    26→   - Which issues will be parallelized
    27→   - Which issues are sequenced and why
    28→   - Which issues are skipped and why
    29→
    30→**Wait for user approval before proceeding.**
    31→
    32→---
    33→
    34→## Phase 2 — Team Setup
    35→
    36→1. Create a team via `TeamCreate` (name: `swarm-YYYY-MM-DD` or similar)
    37→2. Create a task (via `TaskCreate`) for each parallelizable issue, including the beads ID in the task description
    38→3. For each task, spawn a teammate via the `Agent` tool with:
    39→   - `subagent_type: "general-purpose"`
    40→   - `mode: "plan"`
    41→   - `team_name: <team>`
    42→   - Do **NOT** use `isolation: "worktree"` — it is unreliable. Teammates
    43→     create their own worktrees as their mandatory first step (see below).
    44→4. Each teammate's prompt must include:
    45→   - The full issue details (from `bd show`)
    46→   - Instruction to run the appropriate quality gate before reporting done:
    47→     - Go changes → `make api-test-unit && make api-lint`
    48→     - Web changes → `cd web && pnpm build && pnpm lint && pnpm test`
    49→     - Both → `make test-standard`

1→# Agent Instructions
     2→
     3→This project uses **bd** (beads) for issue tracking. Run `bd onboard` to get started.
     4→
     5→## Quick Reference
     6→
     7→```bash
     8→bd ready              # Find available work
     9→bd show <id>          # View issue details
    10→bd update <id> --status in_progress  # Claim work
    11→bd close <id>         # Complete work
    12→bd sync               # Sync with git
    13→```
    14→
    15→## Issue Title Guidelines
    16→
    17→Titles appear in `bd list`, `bd ready`, and terminal window titles where space
    18→is limited. The **first ~20 characters** must be meaningfully descriptive:
    19→
    20→| Bad | Good |
    21→|---|---|
    22→| `Add support for filtering by SAT score` | `SAT score filter for search` |
    23→| `Update the search page to show result counts` | `Search result count display` |
    24→| `Fix bug where geographic radius is ignored` | `Geo radius filter ignored` |
    25→| `Implement new card component for institutions` | `Institution card component` |
    26→
    27→- **Lead with the noun or area** ("Search …", "Auth …", "GeoField …"), not filler verbs
    28→- **Under 50 characters** — put detail in `--description`
    29→- Avoid prefixes like "Add support for", "Update the", "Implement new"
    30→
    31→## Creating Issues
    32→
    33→Use the correct issue type:
    34→- `epic` — a grouping container for related features/tasks. Not directly workable.
    35→- `feature` — delivers new user-visible or agent-visible capability
    36→- `task` — scaffold, chore, or infrastructure work (e.g., project init, CI setup)
    37→- `bug` — something broken that needs fixing
    38→
    39→### Creating Epics
    40→
    41→Epics must use `--waits-for-gate` so they are blocked until their children complete, and do not appear in `bd ready`:
    42→
    43→```bash
    44→bd create "Epic: Feature Area" -t epic -p 2 -l label1,label2 \
    45→  -d "Description of the epic" \
    46→  --waits-for-gate all-children
    47→```
    48→
    49→This makes the epic resolve automatically when all children are closed. Never leave an epic as a bare open issue with no gate — it will pollute `bd ready`.
    50→
    51→### Creating Child Issues Under Epics
    52→
    53→Use `--parent` to place issues under an epic, and `--waits-for` to make the epic wait for the child:
    54→
    55→```bash
    56→bd create "Implement feature X" -t feature -p 1 -l search,go \
    57→  --parent kapow-abc \
    58→  --waits-for kapow-abc \
    59→  -d "Description" \
    60→  --acceptance "Acceptance criteria"
    61→```
    62→
    63→The `--parent` flag establishes the hierarchy. The `--waits-for kapow-abc` flag tells the epic to wait for this child before it can close.
    64→
    65→### Dependencies Between Issues
    66→
    67→Use `bd dep add` to express ordering constraints between issues:
    68→
    69→```bash
    70→bd dep add kapow-child kapow-dependency   # kapow-child is blocked by kapow-dependency
    71→```
    72→
    73→Only add dependencies where there is a real technical ordering constraint (e.g., "the migration must exist before the resolver can query the new column"). Do not add dependencies for soft preferences or nice-to-have ordering.
    74→
    75→## Implementation Plans
    76→
    77→Claude Code saves plans to `~/.claude/plans/` with random names (e.g.,
    78→`replicated-drifting-meerkat.md`). These are local to the machine and lost
    79→when switching devices. **After a plan is approved, copy it into the repo**
    80→so it persists across machines and sessions.
    81→
    82→### Saving a plan to the repo
    83→
    84→After exiting plan mode, copy the plan file from `~/.claude/plans/` into
    85→`docs/plans/`, renaming it to something meaningful:
    86→
    87→```bash
    88→cp ~/.claude/plans/<random-name>.md docs/plans/<issue-id>-<short-name>.md
    89→```
    90→
    91→Examples:
    92→- `docs/plans/kapow-a1b-geo-search.md`
    93→- `docs/plans/auth-refactor.md` (if no issue exists yet)
    94→
    95→Use lowercase kebab-case. Prefer `<issue-id>-<short-name>` when an issue exists.
    96→
    97→### Referencing from issues
    98→
    99→Add a note to the beads issue pointing to the plan:
   100→
   101→```bash
   102→bd update <id> --notes="Plan: docs/plans/<issue-id>-<short-name>.md"
   103→```
   104→
   105→### Resuming work from a plan
   106→
   107→When starting a new session, check `docs/plans/` for in-progress plans.
   108→If a beads issue references a plan file, read it before starting work.
   109→
   110→### Lifecycle
   111→
   112→- Commit and push plan files alongside the code they describe
   113→- Update plan files as the approach evolves during implementation
   114→- Delete plan files after the work is fully merged and closed
   115→
   116→## Completing an Issue
   117→
   118→An issue is not complete until:
   119→1. All code changes pass quality gates (tests, lint, build)
   120→2. Changes are committed
   121→3. The issue is closed with `bd close <id>`
   122→4. Changes are pushed to remote with `bd sync && git push`
   123→
   124→## Git Workflow
   125→
   126→When work is complete and verified (tests pass, build succeeds, lint is clean),
   127→merge directly into `main`. Only fall back to a pull request if there is an
   128→unresolvable problem merging from the command line (e.g. complex conflicts).
   129→
   130→### Commit messages and issue references
   131→
   132→When a commit includes changes to beads data (`.beads/` files), list all
   133→affected issue IDs in the commit message body. This makes it easy to trace
   134→which issues were created, updated, or closed in each push.
   135→
   136→Example:
   137→```
   138→Add geo search field and update filter UI
   139→
   140→Implements proximity-based search using PostGIS ST_DWithin.
   141→
   142→Issues: kapow-a1b, kapow-c2d, kapow-e3f
   143→```
   144→
   145→### Worktree workflow
   146→
   147→When working in a git worktree (e.g. started with `--worktree`), **always merge
   148→the branch back into `main` before ending the session.** Do not leave the
   149→worktree branch unmerged without asking.
   150→
   151→**Before merging a worktree branch:**
   152→- Preview conflicts first: `git merge --no-commit --no-ff <branch>` then `git merge --abort`
   153→- Rebase long-lived worktrees onto main before merging: `git rebase main` (from the worktree)
   154→
   155→**After merging a worktree that touches GraphQL schema files:**
   156→- Always regenerate: `make api-generate && make web-schema-sync`
   157→- `.gitattributes` marks generated files with `merge=ours` — conflicts auto-resolve to the current branch, but you must regenerate to get the correct content
   158→
   159→**Worktree cleanup — do NOT do it manually:**
   160→Claude Code manages worktree lifecycle automatically when invoked with
   161→`--worktree`. **Never** run `git worktree remove` or delete the worktree
   162→directory yourself — the user is prompted to keep or remove it when the session
   163→ends. Manually removing the worktree mid-session can break the working directory
   164→and cause confusing errors.
   165→
   166→## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)
   167→
   168→**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.
   169→
   170→**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**
   171→
   172→1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
   173→2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
   174→3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
   175→4. **PUSH TO REMOTE** - This is MANDATORY:
   176→   ```bash
   177→   git pull --rebase
   178→   bd sync
   179→   git push
   180→   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
   181→   ```
   182→5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches
   183→6. **Verify** - All changes committed AND pushed
   184→7. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session
   185→
   186→**CRITICAL RULES:**
   187→- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
   188→- NEVER stop before pushing - that leaves work stranded locally
   189→- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" - YOU must push
   190→- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds
   191→
   192→
   193→<!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION -->
   194→## Issue Tracking with bd (beads)
   195→
   196→**IMPORTANT**: This project uses **bd (beads)** for ALL issue tracking. Do NOT use markdown TODOs, task lists, or other tracking methods.
   197→
   198→### Why bd?
   199→
   200→- Dependency-aware: Track blockers and relationships between issues
   201→- Git-friendly: Auto-syncs to JSONL for version control
   202→- Agent-optimized: JSON output, ready work detection, discovered-from links
   203→- Prevents duplicate tracking systems and confusion
   204→
   205→### Quick Start
   206→
   207→**Check for ready work:**
   208→
   209→```bash
   210→bd ready --json
   211→```
   212→
   213→**Create new issues:**
   214→
   215→```bash
   216→bd create "Issue title" --description="Detailed context" -t bug|feature|task -p 0-4 --json
   217→bd create "Issue title" --description="What this issue is about" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:bd-123 --json
   218→```
   219→
   220→**Claim and update:**
   221→
   222→```bash
   223→bd update bd-42 --status in_progress --json
   224→bd update bd-42 --priority 1 --json
   225→```
   226→
   227→**Complete work:**
   228→
   229→```bash
   230→bd close bd-42 --reason "Completed" --json
   231→```
   232→
   233→### Issue Types
   234→
   235→- `bug` - Something broken
   236→- `feature` - New functionality
   237→- `task` - Work item (tests, docs, refactoring)
   238→- `epic` - Large feature with subtasks
   239→- `chore` - Maintenance (dependencies, tooling)
   240→
   241→### Priorities
   242→
   243→- `0` - Critical (security, data loss, broken builds)
   244→- `1` - High (major features, important bugs)
   245→- `2` - Medium (default, nice-to-have)
   246→- `3` - Low (polish, optimization)
   247→- `4` - Backlog (future ideas)
   248→
   249→### Workflow for AI Agents
   250→
   251→1. **Check ready work**: `bd ready` shows unblocked issues
   252→2. **Claim your task**: `bd update <id> --status in_progress`
   253→3. **Work on it**: Implement, test, document
   254→4. **Discover new work?** Create linked issue:
   255→   - `bd create "Found bug" --description="Details about what was found" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:<parent-id>`
   256→5. **Complete**: `bd close <id> --reason "Done"`
   257→
   258→### Auto-Sync
   259→
   260→bd automatically syncs with git:
   261→
   262→- Exports to `.beads/issues.jsonl` after changes (5s debounce)
   263→- Imports from JSONL when newer (e.g., after `git pull`)
   264→- No manual export/import needed!
   265→
   266→### Important Rules
   267→
   268→- ✅ Use bd for ALL task tracking
   269→- ✅ Always use `--json` flag for programmatic use
   270→- ✅ Link discovered work with `discovered-from` dependencies
   271→- ✅ Check `bd ready` before asking "what should I work on?"
   272→- ❌ Do NOT create markdown TODO lists
   273→- ❌ Do NOT use external issue trackers
   274→- ❌ Do NOT duplicate tracking systems
   275→
   276→For more details, see README.md and docs/QUICKSTART.md.
   277→
   278→<!-- END BEADS INTEGRATION -->

### Swarm Skill Overview

The `/swarm` skill is a user-invocable team orchestration system for parallelizing issue work. It manages:

4. **Monitor & Merge** — Verifies quality gates, merges branches to main, closes beads issues
5. **Quality Gates** — Runs `make test-standard` on final integrated code
6. **Close Protocol** — Syncs, pushes, shuts down team, reports summary

**Key constraints:**
- Teammates work in isolated git worktrees (`.claude/worktrees/<agent-name>`)
- Each teammate uses plan mode (`mode: "plan"`)
- Subagent type is `general-purpose`
- Worktree isolation is mandatory—first step before any work
- Quality gates required before reporting completion (varies by file type: Go → `make api-test-unit && make api-lint`, Web → build/lint/test, Both → `make test-standard`)
- Branches pushed from worktrees, never edited main directly
- GraphQL schema changes trigger `make api-generate && make web-schema-sync`
- Issues with overlapping files must be sequenced, not parallelized

### Beads Integration (AGENTS.md)

- Check ready work: `bd ready`

- Link discovered work: `--deps discovered-from:<parent-id>`

- Go 1.24+, chi/v5 router, gqlgen (schema-first GraphQL)

- Conventions: Context-first params, parameterized SQL (`$1`, `$2`), no `fmt.Sprintf` for SQL

**React Frontend** (`web/`):
- React 18, TypeScript, Vite, Mantine UI
- gql.tada for GraphQL queries (mandatory schema-first)
- Vitest tests, 80% coverage
- Zero-warning ESLint policy
- Build verification: `pnpm build && pnpm lint` before done

**Database:**
- PostgreSQL 16+ with pgvector
- Embedded goose migrations
- Dev: Docker Compose (PG on :5433, MinIO on :9000/:9001)

### Key Files & Paths

- Root Makefile: Commands for dev/build/test/lint
